query: pathogenesis of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in cirrhotic patients

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Typically, patients who experience SBP have chronic liver disease with a Child-Pugh classification, which assesses the prognosis of liver disease, of C. This ranking involves a high to a maximum score of 10 to 15 points (on the Child-Pugh scale) and measures 1-year patient survival at 45% and 2-year survival at 35%. Decompensated cirrhotic patients are those at the highest risk of developing SBP. Infecting organisms typically originate from the intestinal lumen, from where they pass via translocation to mesenteric lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Some cirrhotic patients with SBP and either a serum creatinine higher than 1 mg/dL, a blood urea nitrogen (BUN) higher than 30mg/dL, or total bilirubin greater than 4 mg/dL should be given adjunctive (i.e., in addition to antibiotics) albumin intravenously. This has been shown to reduce both in-hospital mortality and renal damage compared to the use of antibiotic therapy alone.

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: An alternatively proposed mechanism of contamination involves hematogenous spread, from a distant source, such as a urinary tract infection, in individuals predisposed to the disease by a weakened immune system (i.e., immunocompromised).  Patients with cirrhosis typically have an elevated level of bacterial overgrowth in the GI tract, mainly due to a prolonged intestinal transit time. This, coupled with reduced protein production by a cirrhotic liver (e.g., low complement levels in both the serum and ascites) and poor phagocytic and reticuloendothelial system function, results in a decreased ability to clear microorganisms from the system, thereby further contributing to bacterial overgrowth, migration, and expansion within the ascites fluid. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: One should have a high index of suspicion for SBP in all patients presenting with ascites, and this is especially true if the patient has an acute history of clinical deterioration. The majority of patients with SBP will present with fever, chills, and abdominal pain, although some patients may be asymptomatic, and SBP is an incidental finding. Temperature is the most common symptom encountered in patients with SBP, which is a particularly useful clinical symptom as patients with cirrhosis are typically hypothermic. Additional signs and symptoms include diarrhea, paralytic ileus, new-onset or worsening encephalopathy (e.g., altered mental status) without any other identifiable cause, new-onset or worsening renal failure, or presence of ascites that does not improve with the use of diuretic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: More recently, proposed tests for SBP include a rapid reagent strip that evaluates for the presence of leukocyte esterase in ascitic fluid, which is shown to have a sensitivity of 100% in the diagnosis of SBP when compared to manual PMN counting. Though this may prove to be a much more efficient method of SBP diagnosis than those previously used, the test still needs to undergo a large-scale comprehensive evaluation. Patients who are at high risk for SBP include: Patients with gastrointestinal bleed and cirrhosis. Patients who already had SBP one or more times in the past. Cirrhotic patients with ascites in which ascitic fluid protein is < 1.5 g/dl along with renal failure (creatinine > 1.2 mg/dl). Cirrhotic patients hospitalized for a cause other than SBP have an ascitic protein concentration of < 1 g/dl (10 g/L).

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The infection-related mortality in SBP is very low with appropriate treatment. However, the mortality rate is quite high in those patients who develop sepsis. But with appropriate antibiotic therapy, better outcomes can be achieved. In hospital settings, the non-infection-related mortality in SBP patients can be as high as 20-40 percent, and one to two years of mortality rates are 70 and 80 percent, respectively. [15] Regardless of the short-term outcome of SBP, patients who have liver disease severe enough to develop SBP usually have a terrible long-term prognosis. Moreover, liver transplantation should be considered for the survivors of SBP, who are otherwise good transplantation candidates.

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is a term used to describe acute infection of ascites, an abnormal accumulation of fluid in the abdomen without a distinct or identifiable source of infection. [1] [2] SBP virtually always occurs in patients with cirrhosis and ascites and is suspected when the patients present with abdominal pain, fever, or altered mental status. There are not accepted diagnostic criteria for SBP, and a minority of patients also presents without apparent abdominal pain.

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: SBP can occur in adults and children. In children, it most commonly occurs in neonates and those around five years of age. It is most common in patients with cirrhosis. However, it can occur as a complication of any disease that results in the accumulation of ascitic fluid, such as liver disease, Budd-Chiari syndrome, congestive heart failure, systemic lupus erythematosus, renal failure, or cancers, and has a poor prognosis. Approximately 10 to 25% of patients with ascites will develop SBP, and the condition is associated with a 20% in-hospital rate of mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Renal failure Sepsis Liver failure/insufficiency Tense ascites Bleeding after paracentesis Bowel perforation after paracentesis Spontaneous fungal peritonitis

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Liver Disease -- History and Physical -- Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP)
retrieved_docs.content: It is one of the acute and painful complications of chronic liver disease. Bacteria ( E. coli, Klebsiella, Streptococcus pneumonia ) seep through the gastrointestinal tract and infect the ascitic fluid. The infection spread through the fluid to the peritoneal membrane, causing inflammation. SBP presents with fever, generalized abdominal pain, tenderness, and absent bowel sounds.

retrieved_docs.title: Septic Peritonitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The clinically significant growth of pathogenic microorganisms into the sterile abdominal cavity is most often accomplished by the violation (most commonly perforation) of the gastrointestinal viscera anywhere along the track, which can be precipitated by a chronic disease process and makes tissue friable and more susceptible to rupture, or an acute onset disease (eg, cholecystitis, pancreatitis). Mechanical violations of the abdominal wall are also responsible for a significant proportion of infections, including but not limited to trauma, surgery, and seeding from dialysis catheters. To a lesser degree, an infection can manifest as a result of hematogenous spread in immunocompromised patients and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in individuals with ascitic fluid collection resultant from cirrhosis, congestive heart failure, and nephrotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Liver Disease -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The following are the complications of chronic liver disease. Variceal bleeding Ascites and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) Hepatic encephalopathy Hepatorenal syndrome Hepatopulmonary syndrome Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Additional risk factors for SBP include a previous history of SBP, low complement levels, and reduced hepatic synthesis of proteins with (1) an associated prolonged PT time and reduced protein levels in ascitic fluid (less than 1 g/dL), and (2) long-term proton pump inhibitor (PPI) therapy such as increased gastric pH with PPI use, which promotes gut bacterial growth and translocation. Though in adults, SBP is typically seen in patients with abdominal ascites, most children with SBP do not have ascites. The reason for this has not yet been elucidated. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Cirrhosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with cirrhosis can be asymptomatic or symptomatic, depending on whether their cirrhosis is clinically compensated or decompensated. In compensated cirrhosis, patients are usually asymptomatic, and their disease is detected incidentally by labs, physical exams, or imaging. One of the common findings is mild to moderate elevation in aminotransferases or gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase with possible enlarged liver or spleen on the exam. On the other hand, patients with decompensated cirrhosis usually present with a wide range of signs and symptoms arising from a combination of liver dysfunction and portal hypertension. The diagnosis of ascites, jaundice, hepatic encephalopathy, variceal bleeding, or hepatocellular carcinoma in a patient with cirrhosis signifies the transition from a compensated to a decompensated phase of cirrhosis. Other cirrhosis complications include spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and hepatorenal syndrome, which occur in patients who have ascites.

retrieved_docs.title: Ascites -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ascites is the pathologic accumulation of fluid within the peritoneal cavity. It is the most common complication of cirrhosis and occurs in about 50% of patient with decompensated cirrhosis in 10 years. The development of ascites denotes the transition from compensated to decompensated cirrhosis. Mortality increases from complications such as spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and hepatorenal syndrome. Mortality ranges from 15% in a year to 44% in 5 years. This activity describes the evaluation, diagnosis, and management of ascites and highlights the role of team-based interprofessional care for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptogenic Cirrhosis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of cryptogenic cirrhosis include the same complications one would find with other causes of cirrhosis. These complications include hepatocellular carcinoma, hepatorenal syndrome, hepatopulmonary syndrome, variceal bleeding, ascites, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, and hepatic encephalopathy. Additional potential complications for cryptogenic cirrhosis include heart disease, renal disease, and other potential complications of diabetes, such as retinopathy. Additional complications depend on the underlying etiology of cryptogenic cirrhosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal paracentesis is a medical procedure that is directly associated with الاسْتِسْقَاء (ascites). It is performed to obtain peritoneal fluid for diagnostic evaluation or to therapeutically drain excess fluid in patients who have ascites, thereby assisting clinicians in determining the underlying cause of the fluid accumulation and providing symptom relief.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ). In patients with alcoholic cirrhosis, chest radiographs frequently show atypical patterns that may reflect complications such as pulmonary edema, infections, or pleural effusions, underscoring the importance of evaluating chest imaging when managing this liver disease.

Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected roles of abdominal and retroperitoneal anatomy, liver pathology, toxic substance exposure, detoxification programs, and related diagnostic and therapeutic procedures in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

Overall, the knowledge base illustrates how abdominal and peritoneal surgical interventions, renal endoscopic and lithotripsy procedures, associated diseases, and a spectrum of diagnostic and therapeutic imaging techniques are interrelated, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 195

Clinical findings such as an abdominal mass (كتلة في البَطْن) or acute abdomen (بَطْنٌ حادّ) are evaluated with the same imaging and procedural approaches. Understanding the interconnected anatomy of the abdomen and liver, the spectrum of hepatic diseases, their risk factors, and the potential toxicities of substances used in abdominal interventions is essential for safe procedural planning, accurate diagnosis, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2022
retrieved_docs.content: Exploratory laparotomy and ثقب معوي (intestinal perforation) are frequently observed together in clinical practice. An exploratory laparotomy is often performed when a ثقب معوي is suspected or identified, as the surgical approach allows direct visualization and management of the perforated bowel. Recognizing the co‑occurrence of these conditions is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate operative intervention, which can reduce morbidity and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1228
retrieved_docs.content: Tetradecylsíran sodný, a sclerosing medication, is contraindicated in patients who have إنتان (sepsis), meaning it should not be administered when a systemic infection is present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: SMQ. Hepatic cirrhosis and portal hypertension predispose patients to دوالي المَريء, which can progress to نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding). This bleeding is a major cause of نَزِف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي (upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage) and is linked to نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (variceal bleeding). Overall, the framework links esophageal variceal pathology, its underlying vascular morphology, associated bleeding manifestations, alcohol‑related liver disease, portal hypertension, and the endoscopic therapeutic procedures used for diagnosis, classification, and patient‑centered management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes an integrated clinical framework linking abdominal surgical interventions, renal replacement therapies, kidney diseases, hematologic disorders, metabolic and autoimmune conditions, and associated diagnostic and procedural practices. Abdominal destructive procedures, including excision and lysis of peritoneal adhesions, are performed for acute abdomen or palpable abdominal mass and involve the peritoneal surface, which may also present with abdominal hernia or intestinal obstruction. Renal replacement access is created by external arteriovenous shunts for hemodialysis, a modality of dialysis used in chronic kidney disease (CKD), chronic renal failure, end‑stage renal disease (ESRD), and acute renal failure, and is associated with hypertension (chronic, secondary), microcytic anemia, and other blood disorders. Complications of hemodialysis include acute dialysis disorder, first‑use syndrome, dialysis‑dysfunction (dialysis disequilibrium) syndrome, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: disease include liver biopsy (خزعة الكبد), partially excised liver anatomy procedures, and ultrasound‑guided examinations, all classified under diagnostic ultrasound of the gastrointestinal tract or of the abdomen/retroperitoneum. The liver also functions as an indirect site for non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures. Overall, the network highlights the interrelationships among abdominal procedures, biliary imaging, liver pathology, detoxification therapies, and their diagnostic modalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: The abdomen (břicho) is the anatomical region targeted by destructive procedures such as excision and lysis of peritoneal adhesions, as well as by broader interventions like abdominal paracentesis. Within the abdomen, the acute abdomen (بَطْنٌ حادّ) and abdominal mass (كتلة في البَطْن) are specific clinical entities, and the peritoneum serves as the finding site for abdominal hernia (فتق البطن) and intestinal disorders (اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ). The liver (játra) is a constituent organ of the abdomen and a primary site for a wide spectrum of diseases—including hepatitis (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد), primary liver cancer, intra‑hepatic bile‑duct cancer, hepatocellular carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية), cholestatic or “yellow” liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ), liver failure (الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدَيّ), and toxic liver disease with necrosis (Toxische leverziekte met levernecrose). Hepatitis and liver inflammation are directly linked to hepatocellular carcinoma, which in turn relates to cholestatic
